---
title: "Binary & Discrete Choice"
subtitle: "Modeling categorical outcomes"
---

## Overview

So far every outcome in this book has been continuous. But many of the most important questions in economics and health policy have categorical answers: did the patient reach the HbA1c target? which provider did the household choose? how does a person rate their own health on a five-point scale? Running OLS on such outcomes is tempting but treacherous --- it can predict probabilities below zero or above one, and it forces a single linear effect onto a bounded response.

This module develops the proper tools. It starts with the binary case --- the linear probability model and its logit and probit alternatives, derived cleanly from a latent-variable / random-utility story --- and then confronts the interpretation problem that trips up so many applied papers: in a nonlinear model the coefficient is *not* the effect. We make the marginal effect concrete and visual. Finally it extends to unordered (multinomial) and ordered categorical outcomes.

All of these models are estimated by maximum likelihood, so this module builds directly on the [Maximum Likelihood & GMM](../11-mle-gmm/index.qmd) module.

## Pages in This Module

- [Binary Outcomes: LPM, Logit, and Probit](binary-outcomes.qmd) --- Modeling a yes/no outcome.
- [Interpreting Nonlinear Models: Marginal Effects and Odds Ratios](marginal-effects.qmd) --- From coefficients to effects you can report.
- [Multinomial and Ordered Choice](multinomial-ordered.qmd) --- More than two categories.
